author: @robertleslie6431
text:
I built my own system, studied up first,  and ten 6volt lead acid, deepcycle, 105 amp hour, golf cart batteries, came out the best choice. Wired for 12 volts into a 110 inverter, fed by ten 17.3v panels, everything came out even, and no regulator needed, they lasted my homestead, with 2, 20 amp 110 circuits, all that was ever needed to run my house and all equipment, saws, drills, fridge/freezer, etc. for 20 years without a single problem, until, still working , I sold the property. I did take good care of them, and I dont believe a better set up could be found. 
Now he said these batteries only have a few hundred cycles? They rate at a thoudand, and if you wash them out and replace the electrolytes once every 5 or 6 years they double that easily. 
Hydrogen gas? How many cars have you ever heard of blowing up? None, they are vented. All that's needed is to not be stupid enough to try getting high on it.

author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
One of many jobs I've had was installing Battery Banks for the Phone Companies, because their Uninterruptible Power Supply is to have a Power Supply powering the telephone exchange, while simultaneously maintaining the charge on a rather sizeable bank of 12V usually Lead-Calcium Cells in a Nominal 48V setup. They all shared a Common Buss Bar, as cables were too small for the currents involved.
Needless to say, the Battery was in its own room, VERY well ventilated. We have horror stories of ventilators failing and gas building up in the industry.
I chose LiFePO4 for my initial system, and they're going to have their own house, separate from all other structures, and be ventilated. With these guys, I'm not worried about Hydrogen buildup as I am Thermal Runaway, they DO have Lithium.

text: “YOU KNOW, when you put your hand on an EV-motor after driving, it isn’t even warm. IT’S SURPRISINGLY cool.”

author: @charleso0o0o
text: “YOU KNOW, when I charge my LEAF EV at 1.4kWh it draw 9A from each 300Ah battery tandem. TOTAL CAPACITY is 45kWh.18kW DRAWDOWN IS to midpoint near 26V. TYPICAL RECHARGE after 40-miles is 12kW. ARRAYS SIZE is 2.4kW functional. AMPERAGES OF 45A as maximum upper limit are possible using commonly available components of hardware. A FUELED DC-augmentation source for winter is reliable & practical with neodymium rotor upto 800W output at 28VDC. WITH 45KW capacity in lithium batteries now, there is no need for augmentation, probably. HOUSEHOLD DEMAND is typically less than 2kW per day. EV-CHARGING HAS been pretty cool. 😎 “

author: @charleso0o0o
text: “START WITH metal for safety: AN ENTRANCE enclosure from outside protects wires that enter your structure. ANOTHER ENCLOSURE can be a repurposed breaker switch to provide safety in function. BOTH ARE connected with EMT or a flexible conduit, preferably metal. ANOTHER ENCLOSURE can be a repurposed load center. IT CAN enclose all connections to batteries, controllers, inverter & ground. IS HARD to find a controller type with EMT knockouts for connections, but there is one or perhaps two of PWM type. REPURPOSED LOAD-CENTER resolves problems. YOU KNOW, high current connection surfaces degrade, is unavoidable, but degradation can be retarded, slowed through distribution of high amperage. HAVE MEASURED severe degradation in a 1.5V battery holder. WAS NOT apparent or visible, only measurable. AFTERWARD, I could see that degraded contacts were dull. DISTRIBUTING BATTERY to inverter current can offset surface degradation, a load center provides hardware means to do it safely.”

author: @richardsandwell2285
text: I put my flooded Lead Acid in an old display freezer turned on its side, it helps stop them getting too cold or too hot in summer, Flooded Lead Acid get a bad press, I was at a battery sales counter and they told me a story about a 30 year old Lead acid that still had 80% of its capacity, the old boy had it in is car, he fussed over it and put it on trickle charge regular in winter. My flooded are all scrap and they work fine, they have to I am off grid, I have 15 x 100Ahs in parallel. I watch the volt meter and cut right back once they get to 12.4 volts, I sometimes get one fail, they usually get hot, and drain the others, that is when I swap them for another. I keep thinking I may go over to LiFePo but the recent boat explosion in Northamptonshire has given me second thoughts.

author: @tullgutten
text:
Remember, a lead acid battery can only be discharged about 50% without destroying it even faster. So a 100 AH battery is actually 50 AH.

And the round trip efficiency is terrible for lead while lithium is around or above 95%

Comparing how many lead acid batteries you need to replace over the time compared to lithium and especially lithium iron phosphate.
Then the lead ones is much more expensive and you need more solar to overcome the charge and discharge losses

author: @GerbenWulff
text:
I think you know what you are talking about, but you formulate some things incorrectly.
Flooded lead acid batteries can last a long time. The problem they have is that compared to some lithium ion batteries they have a fairly low cycle life. And the deeper you discharge them, the lower the cycle life. If you do not intend to cycle your battery on a daily basis, then lead acid is a proven and cost-effective technology.
Voltage drop isn't specific for DC current although you make it sound like it is. Voltage drop for DC and AC current is similar, what is different is that for AC current we typically use higher voltages, so the  relative voltage drop is low. If you use a low voltage DC battery, like 12 V, then the voltage drop is relatively high. E.g. if you lose 1 V in a 110 V system, that doesn't matter that much, if you lose that 1 V in a 12 V system, it's a significant loss.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thank you mate - I know both of those things. However, you may have missed the point. It's not that I formulate them incorrectly - I put in them in a general way - like building blocks. In those terms it doesn't matter much which actually battery fills the block of 'battery'. I don't mean to be rude mate but I had to laugh about the bit about voltage drop - i was talking about DC and a system where it is all DC - why would I mention AC?

author: @kevinroberts781
text: Be careful when putting LA batteries in parallel. If they are used and of different degradations they won't last very long at all. Plates will start to burn up in the cells and the whole system will fail in short order. It's best to use the same LA battery as a new cell if you plan on putting them together.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Actually that's true of any kind of battery mate. But mostly when a put a lot of them together - it's why balance boards have become popular - especially in lithium set ups

author: @technicalfool
text:
Figure I'd mention there's some lovely RGB bulbs you can get now, only about 6 watts a piece, but they have their own battery in them and an IR controller. Not the brightest but also not overdriven to the degree that they blow quickly. They magnetically clamp onto their E27 base and can also charge from USB. Combined with a small LiFePO4 Bluetti-brand thingy, I've had the odd power outage or two and not even known about it except for all the alarms in the street going off!

Combine that with the hundred watt foldable solar and a bunch of slowly-acquired pocket power packs and 18650 boxes, basically my small-devices and lighting requirements are more or less met, albeit not as nicely bolted in as yours is going to be. Good for keeping phones, tablets, radios and maybe the occasional small kettle of water available for "free" though.

Going to be fun to see what you end up doing with that setup when it comes to plugging in DIY wind generators and other funky stuff in to see how well it does.

author: @Mrjrich37055
text:
I worked for a battery distributor many years ago and one of my jobs was pouring a new post on batteries that had been damaged. 
One day my boys was out of town so I was out in the warehouse and not in my regular role. A customer came in and had a big heavy machine battery and wanted me to pour him a new post,
A job I have done hundreds of times.
So I put my PPE on and grab a mold and put it in place and grabbed a stick of lead, lit the torch to melt the lead rod and I put my torch down towards the mold and the next thing I know the whole warehouse shook. The battery blew up and the customer decided to just by a new battery. For several years I found little bits of that battery all over the warehouse.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are lucky you didn't die mate!

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text: By post, you mean one of the protruding battery terminals, on the lead acid batteries?

replies.author: @ElizabethGreene
replies.text: @yasirrakhurrafat1142 Yes.  If you disconnect and reconnect a lot, or if the new guy gets overzealous with the post cleaner, then they can fail.  You can put a little tapered mold on them and pour a new lead post instead of replacing the battery.

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:

author: @RePeteAndMe
text:
6:20 "batteries and supercapacitors together"
Yep. Vehicles benefit from tribrid configurations:
Synfuel, biofuel, or, in a pinch fossil fuels for long term power generation.
Batteries for short trips and to help with acceleration, braking, and long term power. An overweight jack of all trades.
Supercaps for acceleration and braking, hopefully without much help from the battery.

9:05 "48 volts"
That's still "low voltage regulations", right?
48 to 12 volt converters are cheap and efficient.
Ebikes generally use 48 volts.
But yeah, four used car batteries that surely don't match in resistance or capacity, series would be asking for trouble.

I invented a battery cell balancing system. It actively rebalances an aging battery without having to replace the weak cells. There's less need to match cells when building the battery. And efficiency improves when cells stay balanced. Let me know if you'd like to see it.

replies.author: @corydwagner
replies.text:
Also, a LiFePO4 battery can be fully discharged without damaging it (although keeping it above 20 percent extends longevity). Lead acid will be permanently damaged if fully discharged. You generally want to keep them above 50 percent SOC. Also, lead acid batteries don't have onboard battery-management systems, which has all kinds of implications for performance, safety, etc.

If you have lead acid batteries sitting around, cool. Use it. But people should just be aware that they have lots of disadvantages.

author: @rafalklepinski7372
text:
Very interesting. Wasn't aware LTO was a safer and longer lasting chemistry. As a blanket safety net I avoided all types of lithium for well-known reasons. Currently running Pb AGM. Ironically here Pb AGM batteries cost _more_ than lithium types so they're sold everywhere but I have severe reservations about using any L type in my home. On a side-note, I get my AGM batteries from an auto scrap yard where they're available at 25% the cost of new ones. Yes they're not "solar deep cycle" rated but in my application they're used more as buffer capacitors for cloudy moments, not for overnight discharging, so stay above 80% SOC always.

Looking forward to some of those DIY batteries you mentioned! :)

replies.author: @jeffpitoniak

author: @alaingrignon
text: Rob, I noticed that in your battery box all the battery connecting wires seem to be roughly the same length. Is this a consideration when wiring them up or is the distance too short to make any difference?

replies.author: @kevinroberts781
replies.text:
I think he did that for resistance. If everything isn't matched perfectly one battery will pull more amps and bare more load. We make the cables the same length to limit this. Sorry, not trying to speak for him. He may have a totally different reason. 

With lead acid cells it's more important to make sure everything is matched as close as possible.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That is exactly why I did it mate.

author: @jasonmorello1374
text: One other part of lead acid, is compared to many, they are something more in reach to repair or rebuild for oneself. Compared to the nastiness of lithium and some other specialty materials, lead acid is strongly documented, and most of the info is very deeply documented. Lead also has a good low melting point and fairly high vapor point, so remaking a rudimentary plate will generally work. Sulfuric acid is a bit higher skilled, but not as much as people might think. Also a place where modern plastics help that was originally the domain of glass. I want more on that nickel iron one, as there is less about it, and it could be something that can be refined without as much specialty equipment compared to even sulfuric acid.
